english: Fried Flat Rice Noodles with Mutton

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Kway Teow Goreng Mutton; closest match is the generic fried noodle dish class (FOODON_03311069).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Kway Teow Goreng Mutton; A0FKM (stir-fried noodle dish with meat) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Singaporean Malay noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kway Teow Goreng Mutton weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical hawker-centre single serving for one adult.

nutrient_source.title: Kway Teow Goreng Mutton — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 for the exact named dish. Saturated fat (2.22 g/100 g), trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.11 mg/100 g) were present in the input record tagged to a delivery-platform source at confidence 0.70; these three values are plausible for this dish type and are retained as lower-confidence estimates pending verification against an approved secondary source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Analogous food: rice noodles, stir-fried (flat).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kway Teow Goreng Mutton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: stir-fried flat rice noodles, reported at GI approximately 56 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (Am J Clin Nutr 2021;114:1625-1632). Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (56 × 70.3) / 100 ≈ 39.4, where carbohydrate per serving = 15.63 g/100 g × 450 g = 70.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 314 mg/100 g with low potassium (86 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g), yielding a sodium-to-potassium ratio of approximately 3.7:1 — well above the recommended ≤1:1 for blood-pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g, request reduced soy sauce, and supplement the meal with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the high sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Flat rice noodles carry a medium GI (~56) and the 450 g serving delivers approximately 70 g of carbohydrate, producing a high glycemic load (~39) per meal; low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g) provides minimal glucose-absorption buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict the serving to approximately 200–250 g, request extra vegetables to increase fibre content, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the medium-to-high glycemic load of a full plate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.22 g/100 g (approximately 10 g per 450 g serving) from mutton and cooking oil, combined with cholesterol at 34 mg/100 g and high sodium (314 mg/100 g), presents a cumulative cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request less oil during preparation, choose a smaller portion (≤200 g), and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to partially mitigate the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which directly amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; high-heat wok frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory red meat component, and consider substituting mutton with chicken, tofu, or seafood if consuming a similar stir-fried noodle dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kway Teow Goreng Mutton is a Singaporean Malay hawker dish consisting of flat rice noodles (kway teow) stir-fried at high heat in a wok with sliced mutton (lamb), egg, bean sprouts, chives, garlic, dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chilli paste (sambal). The dish is characterised by its smoky wok-hei aroma, savoury-spicy flavour profile, and soft yet slightly chewy noodle texture. It is a halal-certified variant of the broader kway teow goreng family and is commonly served at Malay hawker stalls and food courts across Singapore for lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, a 450 g plate provides approximately 635 kcal, 31 g protein, 25 g fat, and 70 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high at 314 mg/100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The medium glycemic index (~56) and high glycemic load per full serving (~39) require carbohydrate monitoring for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The mutton component contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid, which are relevant considerations for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is dairy-free and halal but contains egg, soy, and gluten from soy sauce.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact named dish match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are retained from the input at lower confidence (0.70) and flagged accordingly in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for stir-fried flat rice noodles, documented with full citation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kway Teow Goreng Mutton is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this dish. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and no approved secondary source provides complete values for this specific preparation. saturated_fat_g (2.22), trans_fat_g (0.04), and vitamin_c_mg (1.11) were present in the input record tagged to a delivery-platform source (confidence 0.70); these values are plausible for this dish type but carry lower confidence than the HPB-sourced macros and should be verified against an approved source when data becomes available. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 6.8, fat_g 5.48, carbohydrate_g 15.63) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Foodo.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with HPB as nutrient source and University of Sydney GI Database as GI source.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Ensured nutrient_source and gi_source organizations are explicitly named approved sources.

Iteration 3: Removed any residual reference to forbidden sources (delivery platforms) as primary evidence in nutrient_source. Clarified that HPB Foodo is the sole approved primary source for core macros. Strengthened gi_source title with full peer-reviewed journal citation (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625-1632) to satisfy G2 and Q4 approved-source compliance. Delivery-platform values for saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are explicitly documented as lower-confidence estimates in notes and source_match_notes, not cited as approved primary evidence.
